jokes.text: BTS wordplay escalation: 'I love a good BTS' / 'I love a hot BTS' / 'BTS to the BTS, baby!'
jokes.setup: The hosts are promoting a behind-the-scenes segment, abbreviated 'BTS'
jokes.punchline: Ilana sexualizes it with 'hot BTS,' then both escalate into meaningless but enthusiastic 'BTS to the BTS, baby' — the joke being that the acronym has slipped free of its meaning entirely
jokes.explanation: Functional wordplay that leans on Ilana's tendency to sexualize everything. The escalation into nonsense is very Broad City but the joke is slight — it's a promo bit, not a scripted scene.

jokes.text: Heidi announces she's taking Oliver for detox in the Hamptons because 'standardized testing has really stressed him out'
jokes.setup: Heidi is thanking the girls for house-sitting — framed as a normal wealthy-person departure
jokes.punchline: Her child needs a detox retreat due to test-prep stress — the absurd extreme of helicopter parenting delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical jab at upper-class parenting neurosis delivered in one efficient line. Works because Heidi's sincerity is total — she doesn't know it's absurd. Good economy.

jokes.text: Oliver stress-chants 'Times tables! Topic sentences! Five paragraph essay! Five paragraph essay!'
jokes.setup: Heidi has just explained Oliver needs detox from test stress
jokes.punchline: Oliver literally embodies the anxiety — his stressed outburst is a list of mundane academic concepts, doubled down on 'five paragraph essay' for rhythmic absurdity
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'five paragraph essay' repeated twice is the right comedic choice — it's the most mundane possible thing to lose your mind over. Visual comedy (a frantic child) amplifies the joke.

jokes.text: Heidi reassures Oliver: 'Standardized testing does not define you. Nothing can happen in the global economy that will ever make you need to work.'
jokes.setup: Heidi is trying to calm Oliver with a pep talk about not letting tests define him
jokes.punchline: The reassurance pivots from wholesome to 'you're so rich you'll never have to work' — callous, oblivious, and darkly funny
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely well-constructed joke. The setup mimics inspirational parenting language ('testing does not define you') and pivots sharply into the exact opposite of an empowering message. 'Nothing can happen in the global economy that will ever make you need to work' is specific, wordy, and delivered with the gravitas of wisdom — a perfect ironic inversion. Strong economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Heidi spots a helicopter and says 'Whoa! A helicopter? I thought Uber just had cars.' / 'Uber has cars?'
jokes.setup: Oliver calls out 'Hi, Uber, we're out front!' — Abbi assumes it will be a car
jokes.punchline: A helicopter arrives; Heidi's counter-response ('Uber has cars?') reveals she has never experienced the non-helicopter version of the service
jokes.explanation: A two-beat joke with excellent structure. The first half subverts expectation (helicopter vs. car), the second half inverts it further (Heidi doesn't even know cars are an option). The reversal is fast, clean, and perfectly characterizes Heidi's wealth bubble. Economical and effective.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Abbi and Ilana clearly climb through a window or fence to get inside rather than using the obvious gate nearby
jokes.setup: They've just watched Heidi leave in a helicopter; they're now alone at the mansion
jokes.punchline: The line 'You know, there's a gate right there' implies they did something much harder and more embarrassing to get inside when the easy option was obvious
jokes.explanation: Inferred physical comedy — the dialogue line 'there's a gate right there' only lands as a punchline if something chaotic preceded it. Classic Abbi-and-Ilana self-inflicted incompetence. Rewatch value because the visual gag must be funnier than the transcript implies.

jokes.text: Ilana introduces the washer-dryer with game-show flourish: 'Double jumbo capacity washer-dryer, cleaning 25 pounds of your dirtiest dog shit at lightning speed.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is giving Abbi a tour of the mansion's features with exaggerated grandeur
jokes.punchline: 'Dog shit' inserted into an otherwise legitimate appliance description — the vulgarity punctures the performative elegance
jokes.explanation: Works primarily through character voice — this is exactly how Ilana would give a tour. The vulgarity isn't particularly inspired but it's well-placed and quick.

jokes.text: Ilana narrates the bathroom tour in fake French: 'Enter ze master bath.' / 'Now, mademoiselle, to the tour.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is playing tour guide through a luxurious mansion bathroom
jokes.punchline: Adopting a fake French accent to signal 'fancy' — the affectation is transparently absurd and entirely Ilana
jokes.explanation: Mildly funny character bit. The fake French accent as a luxury signifier is a worn comedic device, but Ilana's commitment sells it. Below average originality.

jokes.text: 'Italian marble, Asian marble, Puerto Rican marble, every ethnicity of marble.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is listing the varieties of marble in the master bath, escalating from geographic to national to... ethnic
jokes.punchline: 'Puerto Rican marble' and 'every ethnicity of marble' — marble does not have ethnicities; Ilana's woke-speak accidentally applied to construction materials
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely sharp: Ilana's identity-politics vocabulary leaking into interior design is specific to her character and produces a genuinely surprising absurdist landing. 'Puerto Rican marble' as a category is the peak of the escalation. Strong character integration — no other character on television would say this.

jokes.text: 'And the coochie de gras...' (referring to the bidet)
jokes.setup: Ilana is building to the final reveal of the bathroom tour with theatrical flair
jokes.punchline: 'Coochie de gras' — a vulgar mash-up of 'coup de grâce' and 'coochie,' perfectly capturing Ilana's worldview that genitalia is the apex of any experience
jokes.explanation: A superb piece of wordplay with exceptional economy. 'Coochie de gras' does three things at once: parodies French tour-guide affectation (continuity from the fake accent), creates a vulgar pun on 'coup de grâce,' and reveals the bidet as the climactic item. Highly quotable. Only Ilana would coin this phrase. Justifies the 8+ craft score.

jokes.text: 'Is that a bidet?' / 'You bidet believe it is.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks about the mysterious fixture
jokes.punchline: Ilana's pun: 'You bidet believe it is' — a 'you better believe it' pun on 'bidet'
jokes.explanation: A solid pun, well-timed and fast. The pun itself is fairly obvious once the bidet is on screen, but the delivery and placement are good. Functional and quotable — people will repeat this joke. Structure is clean even if originality is middling.

jokes.text: Ilana on the bidet: 'the water is perfectly warmed to accommodate my anus.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is testing the bidet while Abbi checks dating apps
jokes.punchline: Ilana volunteers extremely specific and unsolicited rectal comfort information mid-conversation
jokes.explanation: Classic Ilana overshare — her bodily comfort is always communal information in her mind. The specificity of 'accommodate my anus' rather than anything more euphemistic is the right word choice. Character-specific but not particularly surprising for Ilana.

jokes.text: Abbi discovers her high school English teacher Mr. Miller on Bumble while sitting on a bidet
jokes.setup: Abbi is casually browsing her dating profiles while Ilana is on the bidet
jokes.punchline: Of all the places and times to find a former teacher on a dating app, it happens mid-bidet-session in a mansion bathroom
jokes.explanation: The situational comedy of this discovery happening in this setting is well-constructed. The bidet context makes the subsequent 'adult' conversation funnier. Good structural use of location for comedy.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Oh, also your Bumble thing is hot. I don't know, I feel like it's a little creepy.' / 'Hell yeah, sometimes in life you need creepy, freaky fucks.'
jokes.setup: Abbi expresses discomfort about matching with her former teacher
jokes.punchline: Ilana acknowledges 'creepy' and immediately reframes it as a positive quality
jokes.explanation: Functionally funny Ilana character beat — her sex-positive pragmatism turning 'creepy' into a selling point is on-brand. Not particularly original but well-delivered.

jokes.text: 'You must swipe yas, you cannot swipe nas.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is pressuring Abbi to match with Mr. Miller
jokes.punchline: Coining 'nas' as the antonym of 'yas' — Ilana has a proprietary vocabulary for app interactions
jokes.explanation: Quick wordplay that could only come from Ilana's dialect. 'Nas' as the opposite of 'yas' is a small but pleasing invention. Economy is high — two words do a lot of work.

jokes.text: Ilana's suggested opening message: 'I need help studying for my ACTs and I'm trying to get a 69.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks Ilana what she should say to her former teacher on Bumble
jokes.punchline: Ilana's suggestion weaponizes the academic context of their relationship into a sex number pun — layered on 'studying' and 'ACTs'
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed double entendre that works on multiple levels: academic vocabulary ('studying,' 'ACTs,' 'getting a grade'), the teacher fantasy context, and the absurdity of using this as an actual opening line. The setup is clean. Could only be Ilana's suggestion.

jokes.text: Abbi responds with 'Hey. Long time.' — to Ilana's elaborate suggestion
jokes.setup: Ilana has constructed a perfect sex-pun opening message
jokes.punchline: Abbi ignores all of it and writes the most minimal, awkward possible message: 'Hey. Long time.'
jokes.explanation: Perfectly characterizes Abbi: after all of Ilana's encouragement and the perfect comedic setup message, she writes something socially paralyzed and near-wordless. The gap between the build-up and the response IS the joke. Maximum economy — two words doing character work. Justifies 7.5 craft.

jokes.text: I'm gonna write, 'Hey. Long time.' / Okay, sent.
jokes.setup: Ilana has suggested the elaborate '69' message
jokes.punchline: Abbi ignores all of it and sends the most banal possible two-word message, then immediately acts like business is concluded
jokes.explanation: Classic Abbi-vs-Ilana dynamic in two words. After all of Ilana's chaotic energy, Abbi sends the most terse, awkward possible opener and immediately moves on. 'Okay, sent' as a closer is perfect. The comedic economy is exceptional — the contrast does all the work.

jokes.text: Mr. Miller replies almost instantly with 'I'm in town, do you want to meet up tonight?' — Abbi reacts with a scream/gasp, and Ilana asks: 'Is that about the message or the bidet?'
jokes.setup: Abbi screams when she reads Mr. Miller's fast reply
jokes.punchline: Ilana can't tell if the reaction is to the dating news or the ongoing bidet sensation — the callback to the bidet running gag creates a double meaning for Abbi's physical reaction
jokes.explanation: A strong structural joke: the bidet, now established as a running physical comedy element, creates genuine ambiguity about Abbi's involuntary reaction. Ilana's question is delivered as if both are equally plausible outcomes. The callback elevates a simple setup/reaction into something with layers. Rewatch value because you look for the ambiguity.

jokes.text: 'Honestly, both.' / 'He should come here. This place is so adult.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks whether the reaction is to the message or the bidet
jokes.punchline: 'Honestly, both' — Abbi confirms the bidet is genuinely affecting her, then pivots to social planning without missing a beat
jokes.explanation: 'Honestly, both' is a two-word punchline that confirms the absurdity and moves on — very efficient. The contrast between 'I'm being stimulated by a bidet' and 'this place is so adult' is a nice ironic juxtaposition.

jokes.text: Ilana on the bidet: 'I feel like a wine glass fresh out of the dishwasher.'
jokes.setup: Ilana tells Abbi to sit her 'sweet ass on this bidet' to prep for the date
jokes.punchline: Ilana describes the bidet sensation via a pristine-domestic metaphor that is simultaneously elegant and completely undignified
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. 'A wine glass fresh out of the dishwasher' is an inspired, original metaphor for anal hygiene — it's clinical in its cleanliness, domestic, aspirational, and absurd simultaneously. The specificity ('wine glass,' not just 'glass') adds a class signifier that fits the mansion setting. Highly quotable, genuinely surprising, and could only be Ilana. This clears the 8+ bar comfortably.

jokes.text: Ilana's philosophical stance on Lincoln: 'it is an essential part of my character that I'm just not into labels. So we're just enjoying the ride.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks what's going on with Lincoln now that they're official
jokes.punchline: Ilana frames her commitment-phobia as a core personality trait ('essential part of my character') — the self-awareness is actually a form of self-deception
jokes.explanation: Character comedy that does work for Ilana's arc. The self-conscious framing of relationship avoidance as personality branding is recognizable and specific to her. Functional but not a standout joke.

jokes.text: Jaimé arrives and announces he had an adult circumcision and 'cannot get hard for two more weeks'
jokes.setup: Jaimé greets Ilana warmly — clearly a close friend dropping by casually
jokes.punchline: He immediately reveals highly personal surgical information as if it were a normal greeting update
jokes.explanation: Effective introduction of a running gag engine. The overshare is well-paced, Jaimé's nonchalance about his circumcision is characterful, and it immediately establishes the comedy conflict: a man who must not get aroused in an arousing environment.

jokes.text: Abbi asks 'Why can't you get hard?' / Ilana explains 'Cause his stitches will tear. My penis.' — then Abbi says 'Duh. Oh, obviously.'
jokes.setup: Abbi doesn't understand Jaimé's restriction; Ilana explains
jokes.punchline: 'My penis' — Ilana corrects herself mid-sentence as if she has proprietary claim to it; Abbi pretends she knew ('obviously') when she obviously didn't
jokes.explanation: The possessive 'My penis' is excellent character work — Ilana's intimate relationship with Jaimé expressed through ownership language for his genitals. Abbi's 'oh, obviously' face-saving is a strong secondary beat. Two jokes stacked efficiently.

jokes.text: Jaimé: 'I cannot be around vibrations in my current state.'
jokes.setup: Something nearby is vibrating (presumably the bidet or another appliance)
jokes.punchline: Post-circumcision Jaimé is now sensitive to vibrations — an extension of the 'cannot get hard' joke into a specific medical restriction that sounds absurd
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Jaimé circumcision running gag. 'Vibrations' as a trigger is a specific and funny medical-comedy detail. The running gag is doing its job.

jokes.text: Ilana gives Jaimé the bathroom tour: 'Italian marble, Asian marble, Puerto Rican marble... the coochie de gras.' — cut to: 'A bidet is synonymous with sex in rich Guatemala.'
jokes.setup: Ilana repeats her earlier tour for Jaimé
jokes.punchline: The callback to the marble ethnicity bit gets a new twist: Ilana adds 'A bidet is synonymous with sex in rich Guatemala' — a made-up cultural fact delivered with total conviction
jokes.explanation: 'A bidet is synonymous with sex in rich Guatemala' is a beautifully absurd invented cultural fact. The specificity of 'rich Guatemala' — not just Guatemala, but wealthy Guatemala — adds a layer of class-consciousness to the nonsense. The callback structure rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Jaimé: 'A boner is inevitable, I cannot be in here.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just called the bidet the 'coochie de gras' and invoked sex in rich Guatemala
jokes.punchline: Jaimé, medically forbidden from erections, matter-of-factly announces that one is coming and flees
jokes.explanation: Solid payoff to the setup of the tour's erotic naming. The comedy of someone fleeing from their own anatomy is well-executed. Running gag paying off on schedule.

jokes.text: Abbi's fumbling introduction: 'And this is Lincoln, he is a... a man, as well. Friend from way back when. Pal and a confidant. Former teacher. He's not my teacher anymore. We're on a date now.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is introducing the assembled group to each other
jokes.punchline: Her stream-of-consciousness introduction keeps adding unnecessary, panic-driven information — 'former teacher' is buried, then 'we're on a date now' just blurted
jokes.explanation: Extended cringe comedy that earns its length. Each additional descriptor is worse than the last — 'pal and a confidant' is a particularly funny formal-speak panic. The escalation ends with the abrupt blurt of 'we're on a date now.' Abbi's specific social anxiety is well-characterized.

jokes.text: Lincoln's reaction: 'That's great. Wow, that took a long time... to say.'
jokes.setup: Ilana finally says 'boyfriend' after an agonizing pause
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's underplayed observation ('wow, that took a long time... to say') has its own pause, perfectly mirroring Ilana's — he's commenting on the comedy with comedic technique
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's beat — itself paused: 'that took a long time... to say' — mirrors Ilana's word-pause structurally and shows excellent comedic awareness from the writers. It's deadpan, efficient, and earns the laugh from the residue of the previous beat. The pair forms a complete comedic sequence.

jokes.text: Jaimé wanders into 'the taxidermy room' and says 'Oh. Nothing. Hi.' as if he's been caught doing something
jokes.setup: Jaimé has been missing; he reappears from a taxidermy room
jokes.punchline: His 'Oh. Nothing. Hi.' implies either he was doing something with the taxidermied animals or he's been hiding from his own erection in there — both are funnier than the truth
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag. A taxidermy room is inherently funny as a random mansion detail, and Jaimé's guilty 'Oh. Nothing. Hi.' is a strong reaction beat. The rewatch value is in wondering what he was actually doing.

jokes.text: Jaimé: 'He used to be your teacher? And now you're on a date?' / 'Yes.' / Jaimé stubs his penis somehow and exits.
jokes.setup: Jaimé learns Richard was Abbi's teacher; the revelation appears to arouse him
jokes.punchline: Jaimé finds the teacher-student dynamic hot enough that his circumcision injury activates — 'Ow! Can't... touch.'
jokes.explanation: The running gag gets a new trigger: even the concept of a teacher-student dynamic is enough to threaten Jaimé's stitches. Good escalation within the gag.

jokes.text: Jaimé accidentally touches something, exclaims 'Ow! Can't... touch. I'm so sorry.' and retreats
jokes.setup: Jaimé is trying to navigate the social situation while also protecting his post-circumcision anatomy
jokes.punchline: Any accidental contact causes him pain — the physical comedy of someone for whom normal social interaction (a handshake? a pat?) is medically dangerous
jokes.explanation: The Jaimé subplot continues to escalate. The running gag of his body being a minefield is well-sustained. 'Can't... touch' delivered while in pain is good physical comedy. The callback structure is working well.

jokes.text: Abbi and Richard in the library: 'Yeah, you're right. I am an adult now. Supposably.' / 'Supposedly. With a D.' / 'Sorry. English teacher mode.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been asserting they're both adults; she tries to say 'supposedly' and mispronounces it
jokes.punchline: Richard can't help correcting 'supposably' — the correction proves Abbi's adult claim is undercut by the very thing she said. His instant reflexive teaching mode undercuts the date vibe.
jokes.explanation: Structurally elegant: Abbi is trying to claim adulthood and mispronounces the word for 'in the way that adults do things.' Richard's correction is involuntary and immediate. The joke is self-defeating in the best way — it proves the power dynamic is still active. Strong character integration and irony.

jokes.text: Abbi's revenge-fantasy about Tina Palmer, who wouldn't let her sit at the lunch table 'because like, I wasn't hot enough'
jokes.setup: Richard says Abbi was cuter than the girl he used to correct
jokes.punchline: Abbi is clearly still wounded by a lunch table slight from years ago — she relishes the teacher's validation as the closure she needed from middle school
jokes.explanation: Recognizable cringe-comedy of adult neuroses traced to adolescent wounds. Functional but familiar territory. The specific detail of 'lunch table' is good; not particularly elevated.

jokes.text: Jaimé appears, announcing: 'I have to go piss... hard.' then asks Ilana to zip him up.
jokes.setup: The romantic library scene is interrupted
jokes.punchline: 'I have to go piss... hard' — Jaimé's erection problem gets a new euphemism; the pause before 'hard' is performative gold
jokes.explanation: The pause structure mirrors joke #29's 'boy...friend?' — clever rhythmic echo. 'Piss hard' is an elegantly vulgar euphemism. The zip-up request lands as Ilana's domestic comfort with Jaimé's anatomy.

jokes.text: Ilana zips Lincoln's pants: 'Your fingers are for two things. Filling cavities, and filling cavities.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln thanks Ilana for zipping him up; she responds as if it's a profound statement about his hands
jokes.punchline: 'Filling cavities, and filling cavities' — dentistry and sex, stated as if they're two different things but using the exact same phrase, so the distinction collapses
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay. The double-entendre isn't just 'cavities' meaning vagina — the joke is that she uses the identical phrase twice, implying no distinction between his professional and sexual roles. Economy is very high; this lands in one sentence. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: He said he thought about me like that. Like, when I was his student, though. / Yeah. / Really? / Bitch, durr.
jokes.setup: Abbi is worried that Richard's comment about thinking about her 'like that' referred to when she was his student
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'Bitch, durr' — treating Abbi's horrified realization as obvious — launches into the extended teacher-masturbation theory
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the extended teacher speech. 'Bitch, durr' as Ilana's response to something Abbi finds disturbing treats the disturbing thing as common knowledge, which is the correct comic framing for what follows. The setup-to-theory transition is efficient.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Once students have flowered, teachers are either fucking them or jerking off to them.'
jokes.setup: The teacher-fantasy debate continues
jokes.punchline: 'Flowered' — Ilana uses a euphemism so delicate ('flowered') immediately followed by the most direct possible language ('fucking them or jerking off to them') — the tonal whiplash is the joke
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash between 'flowered' and 'fucking/jerking off' is a deliberate and effective comedic technique — the euphemism makes the bluntness hit harder. Distinctly Ilana's voice.

jokes.text: If it keeps their disgusting hands off my hot-ass, bl-ewish kids, then yeah.
jokes.setup: Ilana is justifying why she'd want teachers to masturbate to her kids
jokes.punchline: 'Bl-ewish' — Ilana's portmanteau of 'Black' and 'Jewish' as a description of her hypothetical children's ethnicity, delivered with pride
jokes.explanation: 'Bl-ewish' is a very Ilana word — she creates identity portmanteaus with the same enthusiasm she brings to everything. It's character-specific (references Lincoln being Black and Ilana being Jewish) and delivered with the pride of someone describing a luxury product. 'Hot-ass, bl-ewish kids' is a specific, absurd phrase that works because of the juxtaposition of maternal pride with inappropriate objectification of hypothetical children. High character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'If Lincoln and I ever have children — and believe me, we're not talking about that for a couple of years — I hope their teachers are jerking off to them.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is escalating her argument that teacher masturbation protects students
jokes.punchline: She applies the logic to her own hypothetical children — which makes the dark argument suddenly and absurdly personal. Also: the parenthetical 'we're not talking about that for a couple of years' is a relationship status update casually buried in a rant about teacher masturbation.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. Two jokes in one: (1) the escalation to personal stakes — hoping for this outcome for her own future children — pushes the dark argument to its logical extreme; (2) the buried parenthetical about not having kids 'for a couple of years' is a relationship-status update delivered as a footnote in the middle of an outrageous argument. The parenthetical alone is a masterclass in how Ilana reveals herself through distraction. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Abbi confesses she's been masturbating to Jonathan Taylor Thomas 'as he was on Home Improvement' — not as a 31-year-old
jokes.setup: Ilana asks if Abbi has ever masturbated to a celebrity 'teen' to normalize the behavior
jokes.punchline: Abbi's horror when she realizes she's been fantasizing about JTT as a teenager — 'Oh, no. I've been jerking off to a teenager every other night.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection: Ilana's question is meant to demonstrate that everyone has this kind of fantasy, but the follow-up question ('as he was on Home Improvement, or as he is now, a 31-year-old?') creates a trap that Abbi walks straight into. 'I've been jerking off to a teenager every other night' is Abbi's horror confronting the exact thing she was judging. The turn works because the viewer makes the same realization Abbi does.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'We're all jerking off to teenagers. And I'll tell you what, they're jerking off to us too. That's the circle of life.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is mortified by her JTT revelation
jokes.punchline: Ilana invokes The Lion King's 'circle of life' as the philosophical framework for intergenerational masturbation — the grandiosity of the reference is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'The circle of life' applied to mutual intergenerational fantasy is an absurdist/dark-subversive punchline with significant quotability. The Disney reference gives the transgressive idea an air of wholesome authority. Very Ilana.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Jerking off is all we got. It literally saves the world.' / Abbi: 'Thank you.'
jokes.setup: The argument has reached its climax
jokes.punchline: Ilana's conclusion — masturbation as world-saving act — and Abbi's sincere 'thank you' as if she's been given genuine wisdom
jokes.explanation: The coda to the argument. Ilana's grandiosity and Abbi's genuine gratitude make the ending of the sequence feel both absurd and warm — their friendship is the real joke. The simplicity of 'Thank you' as a response to 'jerking off saves the world' is perfectly underplayed.

jokes.text: Lincoln farts in front of Ilana and apologizes; Ilana responds: 'Farts are part of the total relationship package.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln and Ilana are having a relationship moment; a fart happens
jokes.punchline: Ilana frames the fart as an inclusive relationship benefit package — 'total relationship package' is corporate language applied to farting
jokes.explanation: Decent joke with good corporate-speak language applied to bathroom humor. The 'total relationship package' framing is efficient. Not surprising but well-delivered.

jokes.text: Ilana farts competitively back at Lincoln — framed as a 'breakdance battle, but not at all'
jokes.setup: Ilana has accepted Lincoln's fart; she then demonstrates her own
jokes.punchline: 'This is like a breakdance battle, but not at all' — Lincoln's own narration undercuts the absurd competitive framing he's invented
jokes.explanation: The self-deprecating meta-description ('but not at all') is good. The visual of a fart battle narrated as a breakdance battle is funny in execution. Above-average gag.

jokes.text: 'That was different from the others.' — Lincoln on a notably different-sounding fart
jokes.setup: Multiple farts have occurred; one is clearly worse
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's understatement — 'that was different from the others' — is the most controlled possible response to an alarming development
jokes.explanation: Beautiful deadpan economy — six words, maximum implication. Lincoln's calm assessment of a crisis is perfectly calibrated. Strong economy score.

jokes.text: Lincoln tells Ilana: 'You shat yourself at a party five weeks ago.' — as perspective on the fart situation
jokes.setup: Lincoln needs to give Ilana perspective on the fart situation
jokes.punchline: He invokes an apparently real historical event — a party five weeks ago — with the precision of someone who tracked this information
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'five weeks ago' is what elevates this joke — Lincoln has filed this information. The casual delivery of genuinely catastrophic information as a normalizing comparison is excellent deadpan. Shows Lincoln and Ilana's relationship has history of exactly this kind.

jokes.text: Richard on the wine: 'sort of a full body with a tannic grip.' / Abbi: 'Yeah, I love full bodies with tannic grips... I love those things with my wine.'
jokes.setup: Richard is describing the wine with expertise; Abbi agrees
jokes.punchline: Abbi repeats the phrase back with increasing uncertainty, then reveals she had no idea what she was agreeing to: 'I love those things with my wine'
jokes.explanation: Well-built cringe sequence. The misdirection is in Abbi's confident agreement, which slowly falls apart — 'I love those things with my wine' is the collapse of the bluff. The tailing off of her performance shows Abbi is out of her depth while trying to perform adulthood. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Sorry about those last few things I said. That sort of came out weird. / If we met right now, you'd just be, like, a hot dude who happens to teach.
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to reset after the awkward teacher-correction moment
jokes.punchline: 'A hot dude who happens to teach' — Abbi's reframing imagines Richard as a stranger, which inadvertently reduces him to a physical description and a job title, not much better
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe continuation. The reframe is sweet in intent but slightly hollow — Abbi is trying too hard to normalize things. Serviceable but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Lincoln and Ilana discover 'his and hers casket sheets'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Lincoln are exploring the master bedroom
jokes.punchline: 'His and hers casket sheets' — the ultra-rich have matched burial linens on their bed; the darkness of the joke is that this exists as a product
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist detail about wealth pathology. 'His and hers casket sheets' as an aspirational luxury item is darkly funny and efficiently delivered. The observation does satirical work.

jokes.text: Ilana announces they have brunch with Abbi and Jaimé on Saturday; Lincoln says he already has 'golf with Dr. Tims.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is assuming coupledom means automatic scheduling alignment
jokes.punchline: Lincoln already has a competing plan — 'golf with Dr. Tims' — established enough to have a name attached; the relationship now requires logistics
jokes.explanation: Functional plot-adjacent comedy. 'Golf with Dr. Tims' is a pleasingly specific fake commitment. Not especially funny on its own but sets up the Google Calendar beat.

jokes.text: 'Sounds like a great idea. I love Google Calendar.' — delivered with the enthusiasm of someone discovering this is a relationship milestone
jokes.setup: They need to coordinate schedules; Lincoln suggests a shared Google Calendar
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'I love Google Calendar' with apparent sincerity — a mundane administrative solution is revealed as a relationship milestone for her
jokes.explanation: Excellent character economy. For someone who refused to say 'boyfriend,' embracing Google Calendar as a positive is a revealing step — the mundane digital tool becomes a symbol of commitment Ilana can handle. The line delivery (presumably enthusiastic) amplifies the irony. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Jaimé studying in a children's room full of books; his penis is apparently knocked by books falling
jokes.setup: Jaimé has been hiding in the children's room to avoid stimulation
jokes.punchline: Books fall on or knock his penis; 'Ay, stop it! My stitches! Please!'
jokes.explanation: Physical slapstick extension of the running gag. The children's room setting (he was hiding there specifically to avoid arousal) is ironic, and the physical comedy is implied. Rewatch value in the visual.

jokes.text: A children's book / toy attacking Jaimé in the groin area: 'Ay, Dios mío.' / 'Ay, stop it! My stitches! My stitches! Please! Please!'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is in the children's room and something (a pop-up book, a toy) hits him in the crotch area
jokes.punchline: Physical contact with his stitches causes him agonized distress; 'Ah, hello, Europe' suggests a pop-up globe or geography book is involved
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that depends heavily on visual execution. 'Ah, hello, Europe' is a genuinely funny non sequitur that implies whatever is attacking him has a geography-book component. The escalation of Jaimé's suffering in the children's room is well-structured. Weaker than the dialogue-based Jaimé jokes but functional.

jokes.text: Richard says he reads 'The New Yorker.' Abbi: 'Wow, like, the whole thing? / That's so hot. That's like, super sexy.' / Richard: 'That I can read?'
jokes.setup: Richard mentions reading The New Yorker
jokes.punchline: Abbi is so impressed by basic literacy/longform magazine consumption that she finds it 'super sexy.' Richard's deadpan: 'That I can read?' — calling out the absurdly low bar
jokes.explanation: Good observational comedy about the low bar of attractive intellectual behavior. 'That I can read?' is the perfect deadpan note — self-aware, surprised, and slightly condescending. The joke comments on Abbi's impressionability while satirizing the fetishization of basic competence.

jokes.text: Abbi's internal monologue/fantasy: 'Pretend like it's day one. Damn, reading is so dope.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to re-see Richard as a neutral adult, not her teacher
jokes.punchline: She coaches herself to see him fresh — and her conclusion is 'damn, reading is so dope' — which is simultaneously genuine and embarrassing
jokes.explanation: Abbi's self-coaching is a revealing character moment. 'Damn, reading is so dope' is her earnest conclusion — she's genuinely impressed by him and knows it's embarrassing. The internal coaching format is a neat structural choice.

jokes.text: Ilana wakes Lincoln: 'Okay, so we have to talk about this whole relationship thing. Think about what we have sacrificed to enter this sexless fart party.'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Lincoln have apparently just had sex; she now has relationship concerns
jokes.punchline: 'Sexless fart party' — Ilana's description of committed relationships as fart-filled celibacy is delivered with the gravity of a genuine crisis
jokes.explanation: 'Sexless fart party' is an immediately quotable, perfectly economical phrase that captures Ilana's nightmare vision of relationships. It also callbacks the actual fart battle — making it autobiographical. The phrase crystallizes a comedic worldview in three words. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'When was the last time we even had sex?' / Lincoln: 'We had sex an hour ago.' / Ilana: 'And that's good enough for you?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is questioning their sex life as part of her relationship fears
jokes.punchline: Lincoln establishes they had sex an hour ago; Ilana dismisses this as insufficient — and Lincoln's inability to respond ('I... I... I can't') is a perfect beat
jokes.explanation: Strong character joke. Ilana's inability to be satisfied even an hour after sex, and Lincoln's stunned non-response ('I... I... I can't') — he literally cannot argue — is a great use of character dynamics. The absurdism of the complaint is anchored in real relationship anxiety, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Lincoln: 'Told you I have a refractory period.'
jokes.setup: Ilana wants more sex; Lincoln is defending himself
jokes.punchline: The clinical medical term 'refractory period' delivered as a matter-of-fact defense — a very Lincoln response to Ilana's absurd demands
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's deployment of technical vocabulary as a shield against Ilana's emotional chaos is a consistent character trait. 'Refractory period' is the right word — precise, slightly defensive, slightly professorial.

jokes.text: Abbi and Richard in the kids' room: 'Looks like somebody didn't do her homework.' / 'But I don't know, is there any extra credit I can do to bring my grade up?' / 'I don't think so. Looks like you're getting a big, fat D this semester.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Richard are now roleplaying in the children's room
jokes.punchline: 'A big, fat D this semester' — Richard leaning into teacher-student sexual roleplay with double-entendre grade language; Jaimé is in the room trying not to get hard
jokes.explanation: Functional teacher-student double entendre. 'Big fat D' is an obvious pun but the execution — in a children's room, with Jaimé nearby — amplifies the cringe. The setup ('extra credit') was flagged by Ilana much earlier, so this is a callback payoff. The D pun itself is predictable.

jokes.text: Chess pieces reveal, followed by: 'Looks like somebody didn't do her homework.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Richard have apparently been playing chess or something chess-related; Richard finds evidence Abbi didn't prepare
jokes.punchline: 'Looks like somebody didn't do her homework' — Richard slips back into teacher mode, and the student-teacher framing re-emerges in a romantic context
jokes.explanation: The chess context is unclear from transcript alone but the joke functions as another callback to the teacher-student dynamic. 'Somebody didn't do her homework' is a reliable phrase that gains irony from the romantic context. Sets up the roleplay that follows.

jokes.text: Um, I didn't. But, I don't know, is there any... extra credit I can do to bring my grade up? / I don't think so. / Looks like you're getting a big, fat 'D' this semester.
jokes.setup: Richard has 'caught' Abbi not doing her homework; she asks for extra credit
jokes.punchline: 'You're getting a big, fat D this semester' — school-grade double entendre; 'D' means both a failing grade and a penis
jokes.explanation: A well-deployed double entendre in context. The 'D' grade/penis pun is not original — it's one of the oldest school-based double entendres — but the delivery in this specific context (teacher-student date roleplay in a mansion) gives it some renewed charge. The setup is efficient. Low originality score is warranted but impact is decent.

jokes.text: Jaimé muttering to himself: 'Jaimé, don't get hard.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is in the room as the roleplay escalates
jokes.punchline: Jaimé addresses his own penis in the third person, trying to coach himself — the self-address as if his erection is a separate entity with agency
jokes.explanation: Third-person self-address is a very funny comedic choice. Jaimé is essentially coaching himself the way an athlete would, which treats his erection as a competitive entity to be defeated. The economy is excellent: three words do a lot of character and running-gag work.

jokes.text: Ilana-Lincoln relationship philosophy argument: 'I like spaghetti, but I don't wanna die with spaghetti. I mean, I might, but I don't know yet.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining her fear of 'forever' in relationships
jokes.punchline: She uses spaghetti as a metaphor for relationships — but then concedes she might die with spaghetti, leaving the metaphor in an unresolved, very Ilana place
jokes.explanation: Ilana's extended food-philosophy metaphors are very specific to her character. The self-correction — 'I mean, I might, but I don't know yet' — is the best part: she's so commitment-phobic she can't even commit to dying alone. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: The Doritos lifetime supply metaphor: 'at first you'd be psyched...then every time you went to grab a bag of Doritos, you're like, oh, I wanna die.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is using food metaphors to explain why 'lifetime' is terrifying
jokes.punchline: A lifetime supply of Doritos leading to suicidal ideation — the escalation from 'psyched' to 'oh, I wanna die' is extreme but logical in Ilana's food-as-metaphor framework
jokes.explanation: The Doritos metaphor is a great extended absurdist sequence. Taking a universally understood 'winning' scenario (lifetime Doritos) and turning it into existential dread is Ilana's philosophical project applied to snack food. 'Oh, I wanna die' at a Dorito bag is a specific, vivid image.

jokes.text: 'Cause lifetime implies death. Think about it like this. Let's say you won a lifetime supply of Doritos. / I'm listening.
jokes.setup: Ilana's argument that 'lifetime' is inherently ominous because it implies death
jokes.punchline: 'I'm listening' — Lincoln's immediate, earnest engagement with the Doritos hypothetical signals he takes junk food as seriously as she does
jokes.explanation: 'I'm listening' is perfectly Lincoln — he's a measured, attentive man and this is his version of being engaged. The Doritos argument is a good absurdist metaphor that somehow works as relationship advice. The two of them taking Doritos as a serious philosophical unit is good character comedy.

jokes.text: 'We do it for a year, short-term. And then after that year, we check in with each other. Like a phone contract.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is proposing an alternative to 'forever'
jokes.punchline: She proposes a relationship structured like a wireless phone contract — an entirely transactional metaphor that Lincoln finds romantic
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. Ilana's phone contract relationship model is specific, original, and reveals exactly how her mind works — corporate and transactional frameworks applied to intimacy because 'forever' is too emotionally loaded. Lincoln's 'I love that' response makes it sweeter. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'And thank you for all the food metaphors.' / Lincoln: 'You're welcome. I was eating spaghetti and Doritos yesterday. That's probably why my farts smell so violent.'
jokes.setup: The Doritos/spaghetti metaphors have been resolved; Lincoln reveals they were autobiographical
jokes.punchline: The food metaphors were based on what Lincoln actually ate, and the consequence is farts — calling back to the fart battle earlier and explaining it
jokes.explanation: A double callback that retroactively explains the fart battle through Lincoln's actual diet. 'My farts smell so violent' is a great specific word choice — 'violent' for farts. The realization that the metaphors were literal is a neat structural trick.

jokes.text: Jaimé's pep talk to himself: 'Jaimé, be strong. Penis, be weak. Be weak! Weak!'
jokes.setup: The teacher-student roleplay is escalating; Jaimé is trying to stay unaroused
jokes.punchline: He addresses his penis directly, with escalating desperation — 'Weak! Weak!' — as if shouting at it will help
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the Jaimé running gag to its peak. 'Jaimé, be strong. Penis, be weak. Be weak! Weak!' is the funniest iteration of the joke — the repetition of 'weak' with increasing desperation, the dual address (himself AND his penis), and the athletic-pep-talk framework is perfectly absurd. Extremely quotable. This is the episode's most viral running-gag payoff.

jokes.text: Richard asks Abbi's age: 'Should we be doing this? You're so young.' / 'I mean, I'm 28.' / 'No, but you're like, 17, right?' / 'Yeah... I am only 17.'
jokes.setup: Richard and Abbi have entered the children's room roleplay
jokes.punchline: Richard is trying to play out the teacher fantasy including an underage element — he asks if she's 17, and Abbi, caught up, goes along with it before realizing what's happening
jokes.explanation: Dark payoff to the earlier conversation about teacher fantasies — it turns out Richard really does have the thing they were theorizing about, and he's trying to enact it. Abbi agreeing and then 'I just got my permit' is a good escalation into cringe.

jokes.text: Richard has been pulling Abbi's skin back 'so I looked more like a child' — her realization and his explanation
jokes.setup: Jaimé has burst in to find Richard doing something to Abbi's face
jokes.punchline: Richard was literally pulling Abbi's face skin to make her look younger — a visual gag about the extreme measures of age-regressive roleplay
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely surprising visual escalation. The image of Richard pulling Abbi's skin to make her look younger is absurd enough to be funny rather than just horrifying. The setup (the 17-year-old roleplay) earns the visual payoff, and Abbi's delayed realization creates cringe. Rewatch value: you look for the skin-pulling in the background.

jokes.text: Jaimé walks in and condemns both of them: 'That, sir, that was just disgusting. I hope you know that. Disgusting.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé has witnessed the skin-pulling roleplay
jokes.punchline: Jaimé — who has spent the entire episode struggling with an erection from a circumcision — is the moral authority condemning Richard as 'disgusting.' The inversion of who's calling who disgusting is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Jaimé as moral arbiter is an ironic inversion — but his outrage reads as genuine. The doubling down on 'disgusting' is good comic rhythm. The juxtaposition of his own situation with his condemnation of Richard is well-constructed.

jokes.text: We were roleplaying, right? Like you're 17. I was trying to make you look young-ish. / What the fuck?
jokes.setup: Richard defends his face-stretching as reasonable roleplay logic
jokes.punchline: 'I was trying to make you look young-ish' — the defense is somehow worse than the act; 'young-ish' is a qualifier that makes it more deliberate
jokes.explanation: The defense is the joke — Richard treating face-pulling as a reasonable romantic gesture when explained in context. 'Young-ish' suggests he was trying but acknowledging it wasn't working perfectly. Abbi's 'What the fuck?' is the appropriate reaction and presumably delivers well.

jokes.text: Abbi to Richard: 'For two, I exfoliate, like, almost every other day. And you know what? My skin is radiant.' — defending herself after being age-regressed by skin-pulling
jokes.setup: Richard has been criticized by Jaimé; Abbi piles on — but her angle is unexpected
jokes.punchline: Rather than criticizing the roleplay itself, Abbi defends her skincare routine — 'my skin is radiant' is her primary concern in this moment
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely excellent character joke. Abbi's main grievance is not the disturbing age-regression roleplay — it's that someone implied her skin looks old. 'My skin is radiant' as her dignity defense is perfectly Abbi. The misdirection (we expect her to address the roleplay) makes the skincare anger land harder. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Jaimé's farewell to Richard: 'That image will forever harden my soul but soften my peepee. Namaste, you sick, sick man.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is dramatically exiting after condemning Richard
jokes.punchline: 'Harden my soul but soften my peepee' — a chiasm (harden/soften, soul/peepee) that is both a pun and a callback to the entire circumcision running gag. 'Namaste' adds absurd spiritual elevation.
jokes.explanation: The best line in the episode. 'Harden my soul but soften my peepee' is a perfectly constructed chiasm — the inverted parallel structure ('harden'/'soften,' 'soul'/'peepee') shows genuine craft. It resolves the circumcision running gag with a verbal flourish that is also a pun on the word 'soften.' 'Namaste' adds spiritual absurdity that caps the dramatic exit. 'Peepee' as the word choice (not 'penis' or anything cruder) is the exact right register. This line justifies a 8.7 craft score: it's structurally precise, characterful, economical, and utterly quotable. The episode's comedic peak.

jokes.text: Richard, departing: 'Look, Abbi, it takes two to Bumble.' / attempts to leave through what he thinks is a door / 'Uh, single file!' / 'Single file? I'm only one person.' / 'Use the buddy system!'
jokes.setup: Richard is making a dignified exit after the disastrous date
jokes.punchline: He gets confused navigating the mansion and his teaching instincts kick in — 'single file' and 'use the buddy system' — to a single person
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment for Richard. His reflexive teacher commands ('single file!' 'buddy system!') directed at a single adult perfectly captures how deeply the professional role is embedded — and confirms that the teacher dynamic was never going to disappear. Strong character integration. 'Single file? I'm only one person' is the right confused response.

jokes.text: Single file! / Single file? I'm only one person. / Use the buddy system!
jokes.setup: Richard and Abbi are trying to part ways; he instructs her in ways that reveal he can't stop being a teacher
jokes.punchline: 'Single file' to one person is absurd; then 'Use the buddy system' doubles down — classroom management protocols to a single adult woman
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback and character comedy. Richard cannot help himself — even saying goodbye involves classroom management instructions. 'Single file' to one person is already funny because it's nonsensical, and Abbi's puzzled 'I'm only one person' is the straight-man line. Then 'Use the buddy system!' escalates to a second classroom command. The joke lands as a complete and satisfying callback to the teacher-mode running gag. The economy is very good — the commands are short and the absurdity compounds. Justifies 7+ scores.

jokes.text: The dryer fire reveal: 'It's a Newton Elite. It's built only to dry satins, silk, or gold-infused fabrics.' / 'Looks like you guys are drying cheap cotton and polyester here.' / 'Fire was inevitable.'
jokes.setup: There has been a dryer fire in the mansion
jokes.punchline: A luxury appliance designed only for precious fabrics was given cheap laundry — 'Fire was inevitable.' The authority figure delivers this with zero sympathy.
jokes.explanation: 'Fire was inevitable' is an excellent deadpan line — the logical inevitability of using a gold-fabric dryer for cotton is delivered as fact. Good satirical commentary on wealth objects being incompatible with normal use. Economy is high.

jokes.text: Heidi's phone call: the dryer fire is revealed to Heidi, who doesn't even know there's a laundry room. 'She didn't even know there was a laundry room. Doesn't affect her in any way.'
jokes.setup: Abbi nervously calls Heidi to report the fire
jokes.punchline: Heidi is unbothered because she didn't know the laundry room existed — she is so wealthy she has never done laundry in her own home
jokes.explanation: Neat satirical button on the wealth theme. 'Doesn't affect her in any way' — Abbi's flat observation captures both the absurdity of Heidi's wealth and her own exhaustion. The callback to the dryer setup from the episode's opening pays off thematically.

jokes.text: Abbi asks Richard directly: 'Do you... jerk off to your students?' / Richard: 'All teachers do. You kinda have to.'
jokes.setup: The episode-long question about teacher masturbation is directly asked
jokes.punchline: 'All teachers do. You kinda have to.' — Richard's casual normalization is the dark punchline to the entire running theme. He says it as if it's professional advice.
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff to the entire episode's thematic thread about teacher fantasies and what's 'creepy.' Richard's confirmation — delivered casually, as professional fact, 'You kinda have to' — is both the darkest and funniest version of the joke. It validates Ilana's theory precisely and makes Abbi's concern justified but too late. Economy is at maximum. The 'kinda have to' — as if it's a job requirement — is the detail that elevates this beyond a simple callback. Justifies 8.3 craft.

jokes.text: Richard's parting wisdom: 'You have to keep reading. Your brain is a muscle. If you don't use it regularly... it goes away.' / Abbi: 'What a righteous dude.'
jokes.setup: Richard — who just admitted he masturbates to his students — is now offering sincere academic advice
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition of legitimate educational wisdom immediately following his confession is the joke. Abbi's 'what a righteous dude' delivered as genuine admiration is the ironic kicker.
jokes.explanation: The episode's final comedic beat uses ironic juxtaposition expertly. Richard IS a righteous dude, in his way — the advice is genuinely good — and Abbi's sincere reaction ('what a righteous dude') reveals that she's capable of separating the weird from the wise. The moral chaos resolves into a kind of absurd wisdom. Strong structural ending.
